claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 9 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, please let me know each of their ages and names so I can assess eligibility for each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hi, thanks for helping me with this! I've got two children I'd like to claim for - Alex who's 9 years old, and Blake who's 5. So they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great, thanks for providing those details! With Alex (9) and Blake (5) both being under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check a few more things about Alex and Blake.

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not working at all)?**

Given their ages (9 and 5), I'd expect neither of them are in paid employment - but I just need to confirm this to proceed.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all - they're just kids! Alex is in primary school and Blake is too. They don't have any jobs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - they're both in primary school. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

(This would be very unusual for children their age, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't get any benefits themselves. They're just 9 and 5 - they're not claiming anything in their own names.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are Alex and Blake in any kind of apprenticeship?**

Given they're 9 and 5 and both in primary school, I'd assume they're not in any apprenticeship programmes - but just to confirm?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not! They're way too young for that - they're just in primary school. No apprenticeships or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, just ticking the boxes! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **one** of these:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - This is the most common situation
2. **Have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. **Have pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. **Have pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources
6. **Have pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? Most people simply need to confirm they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. We're all living here normally, nothing complicated like those other situations.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's straightforward then! Living normally in the UK meets the residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You meet this requirement if **any** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children** - They live in your household
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** - Under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live with me at home. They're my kids and we all live together in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's the most straightforward way to meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. What's the situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for them. It's just me - I'm the one looking after them and nobody else is getting Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things simple. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Where are Alex and Blake currently living?**

Are both children:
- **Living normally at home with you** (the usual situation)
- **In hospital**
- **In local authority care**
- **In a fostering arrangement**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both just living at home with me normally - nothing special. Just a regular family situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful, that's the typical situation. Nearly there now!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex and Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council **not** financially contributing to housing or looking after your children? (This would only usually apply in special circumstances like certain care arrangements.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for them. I'm paying for everything myself - their accommodation, their food, everything. The council's not involved at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's all the questions I need to ask. Let me confirm the outcome for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! Based on your answers, I can now confirm the eligibility outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for **both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| Alex | 9 | ✅ Yes |
| Blake | 5 | ✅ Yes |

### Why You're Eligible

Both children meet all the requirements because:
- ✅ They are under 16 years old
- ✅ They are not working
- ✅ They don't receive benefits in their own right
- ✅ They are not in apprenticeships
- ✅ You live in the UK
- ✅ You live with both children and are responsible for them
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them
- ✅ The local council is not paying for their care

---

### 📝 Next Steps to Claim

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake
4. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### ⚠️ Important Note

If you or your partner earns over **£60,000** per year, you may need to pay the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. This doesn't affect your eligibility, but it may reduce the benefit amount you keep. You can still claim and register for National Insurance credits even if this applies.

### 🔗 Useful Links

- Apply for Child Benefit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Congratulations, and best of luck with your claim! Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex (age 9) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living at home normally; (9) The local council is not paying for his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) She is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) She is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) She is not receiving benefits in her own right; (4) She is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant lives with Blake and is responsible for her; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (8) Blake is living at home normally; (9) The local council is not paying for her accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They are a UK resident living in Manchester with both children (Alex, 9, and Blake, 5) in a normal family arrangement. Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeships, and the council is not contributing to their care. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 9) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living at home normally; (9) The local council is not paying for his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) She is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) She is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) She is not receiving benefits in her own right; (4) She is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant lives with Blake and is responsible for her; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (8) Blake is living at home normally; (9) The local council is not paying for her accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They are a UK resident living in Manchester with both children (Alex, 9, and Blake, 5) in a normal family arrangement. Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeships, and the council is not contributing to their care. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child.
